data.title: Option Care Health forecasts 2026 revenue of $5.675B-$5.775B while maintaining EBITDA of $480M-$505M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Option Care Health (OPCH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The first quarter reflected mixed performance for our business. Adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS performance were aligned with our expectations, but our revenue growth of 1% did not meet our expectations." (CEO, President & Director John Rademacher)
  • "Across our autoimmune and Chronic Inflammatory Portfolio, which we refer to as [ CID ]. We saw a greater reset than anticipated in patient census." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "We are revising our full year revenue guidance as the industry dynamics are more impactful than anticipated." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Our first quarter revenue was $1.4 billion, up slightly over 1% compared to last year." (Executive VP & CFO Meenal Sethna)
  • "During the quarter, we also expanded our revolving credit facility to enhance financial flexibility from $400 million to $850 million." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Outlook

  • "We are adjusting our full year net revenue guidance to a range of $5.675 billion to $5.775 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We are maintaining our full year EBITDA and adjusted EPS ranges with our February guidance with projected EBITDA of $480 million to $505 million and adjusted EPS range of $1.82 to $1.92." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We are adjusting our operating cash flow target to at least $320 million, which incorporates the lower revenue and cash-based EBITDA reduction." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We expect second quarter sequential revenue growth in the mid-single digits with EBITDA sequential growth in the high single digits." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted EBITDA of $105 million was down 6% over prior year, but in line with our expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Adjusted EPS of $0.40 was flat with prior year with an uplift of $0.02 from the year-over-year benefit of share repurchases." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Operating cash flow for the quarter was a usage of $12 million, in line with our seasonal expectations." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "With clarity of those CID portfolio resets, we now estimate an approximately $55 million gross profit headwind for the year." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Q&A

  • "Can you help me to understand the increase in the headwind versus the initial on the gross profit side?" Lisa Gill, JPMorgan Chase & Co: Research Division; "this $55 million now represents... the change in the patient census itself... but then also therapy mix." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "I'm looking at teens sequential growth in 3Q and 4Q and how we accelerate from... 2Q." Pito Chickering, Deutsche Bank AG: Research Division; "we have a normal seasonal pattern... we tend to see sequential growth starting from the first quarter... up to the fourth quarter." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Was it just competitors were processing these folks a little bit more quickly than you guys and you were losing a little bit of share?" David MacDonald, Truist Securities, Inc.: Research Division; "we expect that we lost some of the patients to some of the competitors through that process." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "It seems like this is a market share loss situation... how can you impart confidence... to have visibility into guidance for the year?" Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies LLC: Research Division; "we reaffirm, yes, we lost those patients to other service providers." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Are we still talking about Stelara... Or are we talking about... ENTYVIO...?" Joanna Gajuk, BofA Securities: Research Division; "it's primarily around the shift of the Stelara patient census." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Would you still expect now that there's any risk transitioning into 2027...?" Michelle Foley, Morgan Stanley: Research Division; "no, we don't expect additional headwind in 2026 nor any carryover in 2027." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "Will that kind of just be on the back burner... as you focus on getting back to that stronger revenue growth?" James Kurek, UBS Investment Bank: Research Division; "the short answer is no... We're absolutely still committed to M&A activity." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Sentiment Analysis

  • "It seems like this is a market share loss situation" and "how can you impart confidence" reflected a slightly negative, pressing analyst tone centered on visibility, competitiveness, and the durability of guidance. (Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies LLC: Research Division)
  • "I am not satisfied with our revenue growth momentum" paired with "we remain confident in the strength of our platform" reflected a neutral-to-slightly defensive management tone, while repeatedly emphasizing clarity after the Q1 reset and sequential rebuilding. (CEO Rademacher)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s "we're excited to share updates" and "we are reaffirming the guidance" tone, the current call shifted toward explaining a "greater reset than anticipated" and a revised revenue outlook. (CEO Rademacher)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "We are adjusting our full year net revenue guidance to a range of $5.675 billion to $5.775 billion" followed Q4 guidance of "full year 2026 revenue of $5.8 billion to $6 billion." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We are maintaining our full year EBITDA and adjusted EPS ranges" remained consistent with Q4 guidance of "adjusted EBITDA of $480 million to $505 million" and "adjusted diluted EPS of $1.82 to $1.92." (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)
  • "We had previously estimated... $25 million to $35 million" and "we now estimate an approximately $55 million gross profit headwind" contrasted with Q4 language that the Stelara impact would "pattern out fairly evenly throughout the year" at the lower range. (Executive VP & CFO Sethna)

Risks and Concerns

  • "We saw a significantly higher volume of patients that had insurance plan, benefit design or formulary management changes, doubling the number of patients requiring benefit reverification and reauthorization versus last year." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "Across our rare and orphan program portfolio, we were also notified of launch delays or slower ramp for a few of our rare and orphan programs due to regulatory or commercial launch readiness." (CEO Rademacher)
  • "We expect that we lost some of the patients to some of the competitors through that process" and "there were some denials" described both competitive and payer-driven friction affecting retention and mix. (CEO Rademacher)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a "mixed performance" quarter where revenue growth "did not meet our expectations," driven by a sharper-than-modeled CID reset tied primarily to "the shift of the Stelara patient census," payer-driven reverification friction, and an unfavorable mix. The company lowered full-year revenue guidance to $5.675 billion-$5.775 billion and raised the expected CID-related gross profit headwind to about $55 million, while keeping EBITDA and adjusted EPS ranges unchanged and describing cost actions, commercial reinvestment, and sequential rebuilding as the path forward.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Quanta projects 2026 revenue of $34.7B-$35.2B while raising adjusted EPS view to $13.55-$14.25
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Quanta Services (PWR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Quanta is off to a strong start of the year with our first quarter results reflecting robust double-digit growth in revenues, adjusted EBITDA and adjusted earnings per share, along with record backlog.” (President, CEO & Director Earl Austin)
  • “What ran through everything we presented in our Investor Day was one word, "certainty", execution certainty, labor certainty, supply chain certainty, schedule certainty.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • “On the fourth quarter call, we announced an investment of $500 million to $700 million over the next several years in our power transformer manufacturing facilities and vertical supply chain strategy, which will double our power transformer manufacturing capacity.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • “This morning, we reported first quarter results with revenues of $7.9 billion, net income attributable to common stock of $221 million or $1.45 per diluted share, adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.68 and adjusted EBITDA of $686 million.” (Chief Financial Officer Jayshree Desai)
  • “Based on the continued momentum evidenced by our record $48.5 billion of backlog, the strong performance during the quarter and improved visibility into the remainder of the year, we are raising our full year financial expectations.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)

Outlook

  • “We now expect revenues to range between $34.7 billion to $35.2 billion, adjusted EBITDA to range between $3.49 billion to $3.65 billion, and adjusted EPS to range between $13.55 and $14.25.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • “Our guidance is prudent. It has always been prudent.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • “Any acquisitions we do on a go-forward basis be additive to the guidance that we give… we’re not contemplating any of that in what you see today.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)

Financial Results

  • “This morning, we reported first quarter results with revenues of $7.9 billion… net income attributable to common stock of $221 million… adjusted diluted earnings per share of $2.68 and adjusted EBITDA of $686 million.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • “We gave a good range when we guided in the fourth quarter about our free cash flow… we didn’t feel the need… to change that with the $500 million range that we provided you guys in the fourth quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • “Do I feel like we have greater confidence about being at the higher end of that cash flow range? Yes, I do.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)

Q&A

  • Nicholas Amicucci, Evercore ISI: Asked if Underground & Infrastructure margin improvement was “a pull forward.” President, CEO & Director Austin: “I do think it continues to get higher and we have the ability to operate in double digits.”
  • Andrew Kaplowitz, Citigroup: Asked if backlog gains were driven by large-load awards. President, CEO & Director Austin: “It was broad-based… I expect our backlog to continue to rise.”
  • Brian Russo, Jefferies: Asked about NiSource GenCo expansion and timing into backlog. President, CEO & Director Austin: “None of the… generation… that’s been announced is not in our backlog… you’ll start to see that come into backlog, probably the later half of the year and beyond.”
  • Ati Modak, Goldman Sachs: Asked whether Quanta will “lean into” gas generation. President, CEO & Director Austin: “It depends on the risk… we’re going to build them under contract structure that makes sense for us and the client and the ratepayers.”
  • Sangita Jain, KeyBanc: Asked why free cash flow guidance was unchanged. Chief Financial Officer Desai: “Really nothing to read into that… it’s just early.”
  • Philip Shen, ROTH: Challenged Q1 strength vs full-year raise. Chief Financial Officer Desai: “Most of that was EBITDA strength for the year as well as a little bit of a tax beat that we carry forward.”
  • Charles Albert Dillard, Bernstein: Asked what it would take to do “full turnkey data center builds at scale.” President, CEO & Director Austin: “If our clients push us that way and asked us to do that, we can do it… if we do it at scale, we need to add.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts tone was slightly skeptical, pressing for durability and conversion, including questions on whether margin upside was “pull forward,” when announced generation spend will reach backlog, and why guidance increases were smaller than perceived Q1 outperformance.
  • Management tone was slightly positive and controlled, repeatedly emphasizing prudence and risk management, including “Our guidance is prudent” (President, CEO & Director Austin) and “we’re going to be prudent about how we take risk” on combined-cycle generation (President, CEO & Director Austin).
  • Versus Q4 2025, management messaging leaned more on Investor Day execution and raised full-year expectations, while Q&A stayed focused on backlog composition, gas generation risk, and scaling data center scope rather than acquisition integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1 2026, the company shifted from setting a baseline 2026 outlook to explicitly “raising our full year financial expectations” on “improved visibility into the remainder of the year.” (Chief Financial Officer Desai)
  • Discussion of vertical integration broadened: Q1 2026 reiterated transformer investment and added that Quanta is “nearly doubling our off-site manufacturing, fabrication and logistics facilities… approximately 6.7 million square feet.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • Analyst focus moved from margin steadiness, supply chain, and renewables noise (Q4 2025) toward backlog drivers, interconnection timelines, gas generation risk controls, and whether Quanta can expand into larger-scope data center delivery (Q1 2026).

Risks and Concerns

  • On combined-cycle gas plants, management stressed execution and contract risk: “We’ll be prudent about how we take risk on them.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • On grid constraints and interconnection delays, management pointed to equipment lead times and permitting: “The transformer manufacturing investment matters a ton when you start talking about 36 months on transformers.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)
  • On geopolitics and commodity volatility, management said: “Our guidance contemplates anything like that… I’m not seeing anything that would impact our customer or ourselves at this point.” (President, CEO & Director Austin)

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an execution-led start to a multi-year plan centered on “certainty,” supported by record $48.5 billion backlog and a raised 2026 outlook for revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and adjusted EPS. In Q&A, management repeatedly emphasized risk-adjusted growth—particularly for combined-cycle generation—and positioned vertical supply chain and off-site manufacturing expansion as key enablers for faster, more reliable delivery across utility, generation, and large-load/data center demand.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Elon Musk testifies he "didn't read the fine print" in OpenAI case
data.description:
Tesla (TSLA) and SpaceX (SPACE) CEO Elon Musk told a federal court on Thursday that he "didn't read the fine print" on a term sheet that had been forwarded to him by OpenAI (OPENAI) CEO Sam Altman in 2017 regarding converting the AI company from a non-profit to a for-profit entity overseen by a non-profit.

"My testimony is I didn't read the fine print, just the headline," Musk said, according to Reuters, during his third day of testimony for a lawsuit he filed against OpenAI.

Musk, an OpenAI co-founder, is suing for $150B, according to Reuters, claiming he was manipulated into investing $38M in the company when it was a non-profit, only to have management convert it into a for-profit entity for their own benefit.

Co-defendants in the suit include OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and President Greg Brockman, who also co-founded the company. Microsoft (MSFT), a major investor in OpenAI, is also party to the suit. 

In addition to monetary damages, Musk is also asking the court to remove Altman and Brockman from their corporate roles and unwind the company's conversion to a for-profit entity.

OpenAI (OPENAI) has countered that Musk was aware the company might be converted into a for-profit entity at some point and decided to walk away in 2018 after failing to gain control. Musk went on to found his own AI company, xAI (X.AI), in 2023, which is now part of Musk's SpaceX (SPACE).

OpenAI has also asserted that Musk filed the suit in part to slow down its progress, given Musk's interest in xAI. On Thursday, Musk disclosed that his company has used OpenAI models to train xAI models. 

OpenAI's attorneys on Thursday questioned Musk as to why he thought OpenAI would remain a non-profit after receiving emails from other co-founders discussing how they could profit from the technology.

"I was reassured by Sam Altman and others ​that OpenAI would continue as a nonprofit," Musk replied, according to Reuters.

Both OpenAI and SpaceX are reportedly preparing to launch massive IPOs later this year.

data.title: OneSpan Non-GAAP EPS of $0.39 beats by $0.04, revenue of $65.95M beats by $3.55M
data.description:
• OneSpan press release (OSPN): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.39 beats by $0.04.
  • Revenue of $65.95M (+4.1% Y/Y) beats by $3.55M.

  • OneSpan is updating its previously issued financial guidance to reflect an increase in its ARR expectations. For the Full Year 2026, the Company expects:

    • Total revenue to be in the range of $244 million to $249 million.
    • Software and services revenue to be in the range of $201 million to $204 million.
    • Hardware revenue to be in the range of $43 million to $45 million.
    • ARR to be in the range of $194 million to $198 million, as compared to its previous guidance range of $192 million to $196 million.
    • Adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $64 million to $68 million.

data.title: Parker projects FY 2026 adjusted EPS of $31.20 as it raises organic growth midpoint to 5.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Parker-Hannifin (PH) Q3 FY 2026

Management view

  • “Q3 was a quarter of record performance, enabled by the strength of our portfolio,” said (Chairman of the Board & CEO Jennifer Parmentier), highlighting “record Q3 sales of $5.5 billion,” “organic growth of 6.5%,” “26.7% adjusted segment operating margin,” “adjusted earnings per share grew 18%,” “year-to-date cash flow from operations was $2.6 billion,” “orders came in at 9%,” and “a record backlog of $12.5 billion,” while adding, “integration planning is underway using our proven playbook” on Filtration Group.

  • “We have the #1 position in the $145 billion motion and control industry, a growing space where we continue to gain share,” said (CEO Parmentier), adding, “2/3 of our revenue comes from customers who buy 4 or more technologies.”

  • “Demand remains robust. Orders continue to outpace shipments, and we are on track to finish our fourth consecutive year of double-digit organic growth,” said (CEO Parmentier) on Aerospace & Defense, noting it “represent[s] 35% of Parker sales.”

  • “We are seeing an increase in OEM orders for heavy-duty truck… and, as a result, are increasing fiscal year '26 sales guidance for this market,” said (CEO Parmentier) on Transportation (15% of sales), while also noting “automotive demand challenges persist.”

  • “Adjusted net income surpassed $1 billion for the first time ever,” and “the adjusted earnings per share of $8.17” was “the first time we've been above $8 for a single quarter ever,” said (Executive VP & CFO Todd Leombruno), also stating, “we remain confident in delivering another record year in 2026.”

Outlook

  • “As a result of these changes, we are increasing our organic sales growth guidance from 5% to 5.5% at the midpoint,” said (CEO Parmentier), including “increasing our forecast from 11% to 12% organic growth” in Aerospace and raising Transportation from “mid-single-digit organic decline to low single-digit organic decline.”

  • “Full year adjusted EPS has been raised by $0.50 to $31.20 at the midpoint,” and “we're also raising our forecast for full year free cash flow to $3.3 billion to $3.6 billion,” said (CFO Leombruno), adding for Q4, “we are guiding reported sales to be nearly $5.5 billion,” “organic growth will be approximately 4%,” “adjusted segment operating margins will be 27.4%,” and “adjusted EPS would be above $8 to be at $8.16.”

  • The prior quarter guidance midpoint was lower: “we are increasing our organic sales growth guidance from 4% to 5% at the midpoint,” said (CEO Parmentier) in Q2, and “we're raising EPS to $30.70 at the midpoint,” said (CFO Leombruno) in Q2.

Financial results

  • “Total sales were up nearly 11%… Organic growth was 6.5%… Adjusted segment operating margins were 26.7%… adjusted EBITDA was… 27.2%,” said (CFO Leombruno), adding, “income tax was favorable, $0.18,” and “we repurchased another $275 million of shares… year-to-date share repurchases [are] $825 million.”

  • “Year-to-date free cash flow increased 17% and came in at $2.3 billion,” and “our Board approved an 11% increase to our quarterly dividend… now $2 per share,” said (CFO Leombruno).

  • Segment detail included “North America… sales were $2.1 billion,” “International… sales were a record $1.5 billion,” and “Aerospace… sales of $1.8 billion,” said (CFO Leombruno), who also cited Aerospace “organic growth was 14.2%” and “backlog… reached a record of $8.4 billion.”

Q&A

  • Mircea Dobre, Baird: “has there been any disruption” from the Middle East and “any impact” from tariffs? (CEO Parmentier): “we're not seeing any material impact to demand,” and on tariffs, “our teams are doing a great job managing them to make sure that there's no impact to earnings.” (CFO Leombruno): “We will not recognize any income on those tariffs until we receive them.”

  • Mircea Dobre, Baird: asked if industrial “incrementals… were a little bit lower” due to pricing/tariffs lag. (CEO Parmentier): “No, no delays, no concerns there with price-cost management whatsoever,” while citing “stronger OEM growth” and “weather-related disruptions.”

  • Jamie Cook, Truist: asked about durability of “40% incremental margin” and whether order confidence “has… waned.” (CFO Leombruno): “we like the setup for FY '27,” and “We hold the team to a target of 30% to 35%.” (CEO Parmentier): “orders were strong throughout the quarter… we feel really good about the guidance.”

  • Jeffrey Sprague, Vertical Research: asked about Q4 Aero growth and OE vs. aftermarket. (CEO Parmentier): “the Aerospace Q4 forecast is approximately 9%… we're not baking in any slowdown here,” and “commercial OEM was up 22% and aftermarket was up 14%.”

  • Christopher Snyder, Morgan Stanley: asked why industrial sales won’t catch up if orders stay elevated, and whether distributors are restocking. (CEO Parmentier): orders include items “due beyond this fiscal year… scheduled into FY '27,” and “We're not seeing any real acceleration or any typical signs of restocking.”

  • Joseph O'Dea, Wells Fargo: asked about Filtration Group timing and synergy priorities. (CEO Parmentier): “we still anticipate that we're going to close within the 12 months of announcement date,” and “We did announce $220 million in synergies by the end of Year 3.” (CFO Leombruno): “There is no prefunding… we do not expect leverage to surpass 3 when we close.”

  • Nigel Coe, Wolfe: asked about Texas facility disruption and data center exposure. (CEO Parmentier): “we don't expect this to have any material impact,” and “It's still approximately 1% of our sales… It's growing nicely.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with risk-focused framing on geopolitics and tariffs (“any disruption”), margins (“incrementals”), and conversion of orders to sales.

  • Management: slightly positive, repeatedly emphasizing control and confidence, including “we're not seeing any material impact,” “no delays,” “we remain confident,” and “we like the setup for FY '27.”

  • Versus Q2: the Q3 Q&A introduced sharper external-risk themes (Middle East, “updated tariff framework,” facility damage), while Q2 leaned more on cycle normalization and “tariffs” mitigation without a specific geopolitical trigger.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance stepped up again in Q3: Q3 raised FY 2026 to “5.5%” organic growth midpoint and “$31.20” adjusted EPS midpoint (CFO Leombruno), versus Q2 raising to “5%” and “$30.70” (CFO Leombruno).

  • The Filtration Group message shifted from timing range to reaffirming the long end: Q2 said “close in 6 to 12 months” (CEO Parmentier), while Q3 said “close within the 12 months” (CEO Parmentier).

  • Analyst focus broadened from cycle/regions in Q2 (Europe turn, APAC strength) to Q3’s added attention on Middle East disruption, tariffs mechanics, and facility disruption.

Risks and concerns

  • “There was damage to our facility… which we are still assessing,” said (CEO Parmentier), while later stating, “we don't expect this to have any material impact to overall Parker.”

  • “Talking about tariffs… it continues to be very dynamic,” said (CEO Parmentier), adding the company analyzes changes “on a regular basis.”

  • On geopolitical exposure, (CEO Parmentier) said “Direct revenue in the Middle East is very small,” and “we're not seeing any material impact to demand.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q3 as “record performance” with raised FY 2026 guidance, emphasizing Aerospace momentum, improving heavy-duty truck demand behind a Transportation outlook increase, and continued confidence in price/cost execution amid “dynamic” tariffs. Executives also reiterated the Filtration Group plan—closing “within the 12 months” of announcement and targeting “$220 million” of synergies—while downplaying expected financial impact from both Middle East disruption and the recently damaged Texas facility.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Southern outlines $1.00 adjusted EPS estimate for Q2 2026 amid $26.5B DOE loan agreements
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Southern Company (SO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "As you can see from the materials that we released this morning, we reported adjusted earnings results for the first quarter above our estimate, with year-over-year growth reflected across all our major businesses." (CEO, President & Chairman Christopher Womack)

  • "The demand for power across our electric service territories has culminated in 23 gigawatts of contracted or late-stage load." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack) "In just the last 2 months, we have signed contracts for another 1.9 gigawatts of customer load with high credit quality hyperscalers, bringing our fully contracted large load agreements to more than 11 gigawatts across our electric subsidiaries." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • "I'd like to highlight the recently announced historic $26.5 billion in loan agreements with the Department of Energy that will benefit customers across Alabama and Georgia for decades." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack) "Over the approximately 30-year term of the DOE loans, this lower cost financing is projected to generate cumulative savings of $7 billion for customers." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • "For the first quarter of 2026, our adjusted EPS was $1.32 per share, $0.09 higher than the first quarter of 2025 and $0.12 above our estimate." (Executive VP & CFO David Poroch)

  • "First quarter weather-normal retail electricity sales to all classes were 2.3% higher than the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch) "Data center usage saw material expansion in the quarter, up 42% year-over-year, primarily due to accelerating usage ramps at large load facilities." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

  • "Last week, the Southern Company Board of Directors approved an increase of $0.08 per share in our annual common dividend, raising the annualized rate to $3.04 per share." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

Outlook

  • "Our adjusted EPS estimate for the second quarter is $1 per share." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

  • "We continue to proactively address equity needs that support our long -- our strong credit quality and path towards 17% FFO to debt by 2029." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch) "We project a remaining need for equity or equity equivalents of approximately $1.8 billion through 2030 in support of our capital plan and long-term credit objectives." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

  • "Georgia Power recently initiated the regulatory process for an all-source RFP to procure 2 to 6 gigawatts of new dispatchable generation resources... projected to be in service in 2032 and 2033." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

Financial Results

  • "For the first quarter of 2026, our adjusted EPS was $1.32 per share." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch) "The primary drivers of our performance for the quarter compared to last year were meaningful customer growth and increased usage, including from data centers at our state-regulated electric utilities." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

  • "This was partially offset by higher financing costs and milder weather year-over-year compared to the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

  • "First quarter weather-normal retail electricity sales to all classes were 2.3% higher than the first quarter of 2025." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch) "The commercial class grew 4.5% in the first quarter when adjusted for weather, bolstered by ongoing growth in data centers." (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: "Can you maybe comment on your views? Is Southern interested? Are you in the consortium?" Christopher Womack: "Southern Company is not at a place to make a commitment about building a new unit." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: "Have those renegotiation conversations started?" Christopher Womack: "So I guess I'd say the answer is yes and yes." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: "How is that kind of creating or changing your philosophy around your regulatory strategy" Christopher Womack: "I think the focus for us is more about rate stability." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: "Is there something about Georgia versus your other states" Christopher Womack: "I think it's more about timing, but also, I think it's the other message that we've been communicating that we're seeing this activity migrate to the West." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • Andrew Weisel, Scotiabank: "What would be the timing of when the process is completed?" Christopher Womack: "You're going to hold your breath until the end of the year before we get through that process." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg Thalmann: "Can you give us a sense of how much of that capacity is currently contracted today?" Christopher Womack: "We've set numbers up in the mid-90s in terms of what's contracted." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

Sentiment Analysis

  • "There seems to be sort of a consortium that's formed" and "there's been some views that hyperscalers would be willing to take on some of the cost inflation risk" reflected a neutral-to-slightly skeptical analyst tone centered on execution and cost-risk transfer; recurring analyst focus also pressed on regulatory optics (Georgia PSC elections), timing of the Georgia RFP, and large-load pipeline integrity. (Analysts)

  • "We're off to a strong start in 2026" and "that momentum gives us confidence" reflected a slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, while several answers in Q&A used boundary-setting language such as "we're not at a place" and "we don't get ahead of our regulators." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • "We're in the midst now of building 10 gigawatts" (Q4 2025) shifted in Q1 2026 toward near-term contracting progress ("signed contracts for another 1.9 gigawatts") and financing visibility tied to the DOE loans ("reducing pressure on our capital market needs"). (Company comparison)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • "This was partially offset by higher financing costs" (Q1 2026) contrasted with Q4 2025 commentary that "we secured the labor and equipment for these projects through early EPC agreements and reservation payments" as the discussion mix moved from build-readiness to the quarter’s cost headwinds and financing actions. (Executive VP & CFO Poroch; CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • "We are moving forward to add 400 megawatts of additional capacity uprates" and "This incremental investment is projected to add approximately $700 million to our capital plan" (Q1 2026) updated Q4 2025’s "late-stage discussions to move forward with up rates of up to an additional 700 megawatts" at Southern Power by narrowing to specific capacity and capital additions. (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

  • "We project a remaining need for equity or equity equivalents of approximately $1.8 billion through 2030" (Q1 2026) compared with "a remaining need... approximately $2 billion through 2030" (Q4 2025), alongside Q1 2026’s note that "we sourced an incremental $500 million of equity" via the ATM forward program. (Executive VP & CFO Poroch)

Risks and Concerns

  • "It's -- in this current market, it's not anything you can take for granted" described ongoing supply-chain tightness across "turbines" and "transformers," while management emphasized mitigation via "size and scale" and supplier relationships. (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

  • "There's a lot of conversation... about data centers and large load customers and things like rate stability" highlighted regulatory and political scrutiny in Georgia, with management stating, "we... have a tremendous history of being able to work with both parties of whoever is in office." (CEO, President & Chairman Womack)

Final Takeaway

Southern Company management emphasized Q1 2026 adjusted EPS of $1.32, maintained a Q2 adjusted EPS estimate of $1.00, and highlighted accelerating large-load contracting, including 1.9 gigawatts signed in the past two months and more than 11 gigawatts fully contracted across electric subsidiaries. Management repeatedly framed customer affordability and regulatory durability around bilateral contracts designed so large-load customers "cover their full share," while pointing to the $26.5 billion DOE loan agreements as a long-dated financing lever expected to generate $7 billion of cumulative customer savings and reduce capital markets pressure. In Q&A, analysts concentrated on nuclear optionality, Georgia regulatory dynamics, the pace and integrity of the load funnel, and the timing of procurement processes, while management reiterated that new nuclear is not yet a commitment, the Georgia all-source RFP is a year-long process, and Southern Power growth remains tied to recontracting and fully contracted, creditworthy counterparties.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Reddit rallies on top- and bottom-line beat, strong Q2 revenue outlook
data.description:
Reddit (RDDT) shares are rallying in Thursday’s after-hours on a sizeable top- and bottom-line beat and Q2 guidance that exceeded Wall Street’s expectations.

Fueled by a 74% surge in advertising revenue, the company generated $663M in total revenue, an increase of 69% year-over-year, and more than $53M better than anticipated.

User metrics showed constructive gains as well, as the number of daily average users ((DAUs)) in the U.S. increased by 7% to 53.5M and international DAUs increased 26% to 73.3M, resulting in a 17% gain of global DAUs to 126.8M versus 126.3M estimates. Additionally, average revenue per user ((ARPU)) was up 44% to $5.23, better than $5.05 expectations.

The upbeat results led to a profit of $1.01 per share, an increase of 74% from the same quarter last year and $0.45 above estimates.

For Q2, Reddit (RDDT) expects revenue to be within a range of $715M to $725M, with a midpoint of $720M that exceeds $714M estimates.

Shares are 10% higher in postmarket trading, setting the stock up to trade higher on Friday after three days in the red.

data.title: DBV Technologies GAAP EPS of -$0.11
data.description:
• DBV Technologies press release (DBVT): Q1 GAAP EPS of -$0.11.

  • Cash Position and LiquidityOn March 31, 2026, the Company held $229 million in Cash and Cash Equivalents compared to $194 million of Cash and Cash Equivalents on December 31, 2025. Net cash used for operating activities was $49 million and $20 million for the periods ended March 31, 2026, and March 31, 2025, respectively. The Company’s net cash flows provided by financing activities totaled $89 million for the periods ended March 31, 2026, following the full exercise of the ABSA Warrants and BS Warrants Issued on its March 2025 PIPE Financing.
